titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John McHale archive
titlestmt.author: compiled by Francis Lapka

publicationstmt.publisher: Yale Center for British Art, Rare Books and Manuscripts

address.addressline: Department of Rare Books and Manuscripts

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Yale Center for British Art, Rare Books and Manuscripts

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday March 10, 2020 at 21:00

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Rare Books and Manuscripts

did.abstract: The collection comprises collage books, collages, drawings, palettes, and posters by John McHale, with related printed material, including catalogs, periodicals, ephemera, and small posters.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The collection is the physical property of the Yale Center for British Art. Literary rights, including copyright, also belong to the Yale Center for British Art. For further information, consult the Curator of Rare Books and Manuscripts.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John McHale Archive. Yale Center for British Art, Gift of Magda Cordell McHale

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Yale Center for British Art, Gift of Magda Cordell McHale

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John McHale (1922-1978) was a British collage artist, art theorist, and sociologist. As a member of the Independent Group, formed at the Institute of Contemporary Arts in 1952, McHale helped to originate Pop Art in Britain, a movement rooted in American mass culture and modern technology.

McHale spent a year at Yale University in 1955-1956 and returned permanently to the US in 1962 to work with Richard Buckminster Fuller on ecological issues and environmental sustainability. With his wife, the artist Magda Cordell, McHale founded the Center for Integrative Studies (CIS) to consider the impact of modern industrialized world on human society and the environment.

In a 1984 retrospective of McHale's work, Charlotta Kotik wrote: "The unique oeuvre of John McHale results from both his special creative talents and his exceptional human qualities. John McHale was at once a visual artist, writer, educator and organizer; but most important, he was a humanist philosopher endowed with relentless energy to pursue innovations which were ultimately to result in changes beneficial to mankind. He looked toward the future with a mixture of optimism and skepticism, but above all with a great deal of realistic wisdom, making possible a clear formulation of our predicament, and the consequences of our behavior well in advance of the mainstream." ( The Expendable Ikon , p. 9).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection comprises collage books, collages, drawings, palettes, and posters by John McHale, with related printed material, including catalogs, periodicals, ephemera, and small posters.

bibliography.head: Bibliography
bibliography.bibref: The Independent Group: postwar Britain and the aesthetics of plenty. Edited by David Robbins. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, c1990
McHale, John. The expendable ikon. Buffalo, NY: Albright-Knox Art Gallery, 1984

title.part: McHale, John. Why I took to the washers in luxury flats

persname.part: Mesens, E. L. T., 1903-1971 (Edouard Léon Théodore)

corpname.part: This Is Tomorrow (1956 : Whitechapel Art Gallery)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Much of McHale's works of the 1950s are devoted to (re)processing media information. There is a recurrent interest in the processing of information through communication systems (see McHale's lecture material). His collage books are best understood as efforts to enact/create such systems in a viewer-interactive form.

did.unittitle: Why I Took to the Washers in Luxury Flats

odd.head: General
odd.p: Signed on front cover: John McHale '54

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Use requires permission of the Curator of Rare Books and Manuscripts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "McHale at this time abandoned the neural forms of his earlier work in order to emphasize the role of communications media. He wished to visualize how raw data (represented by torn bits of colored paper, newspapers, and magazines) might be organized by a human or mechanical brain into discrete messages. The collage books that he produced in 1954 (cat. no. 37) synthesized the themes of mass culture, information processing, and viewer participation. Each page of these books (one of which was shown in the ICA's Collages and Objects exhibition in 1954) is composed of a collage cut into strips, so that the volume becomes a bound series of variable collages to be 'read' however the viewer wishes."--Baas, Jacquelynn. "John McHale" In The Independent Group: postwar Britain and the aesthetics of plenty. Edited by David Robbins. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, c1990.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Use requires permission of the Curator of Rare Books and Manuscripts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Shoe-Life Stories (circa 1954) sliced reproductions of faces vertically and alternated these with textures of objects from a jeweler's catalog."--Buckley, Craig. Graphic Assembly: Montage, Media, and Experimental Architecture in the 1960. University of Minnesota Press, 2019, page 50.

"McHale at this time abandoned the neural forms of his earlier work in order to emphasize the role of communications media. He wished to visualize how raw data (represented by torn bits of colored paper, newspapers, and magazines) might be organized by a human or mechanical brain into discrete messages. The collage books that he produced in 1954 (cat. no. 37) synthesized the themes of mass culture, information processing, and viewer participation. Each page of these books ... is composed of a collage cut into strips, so that the volume becomes a bound series of variable collages to be 'read' however the viewer wishes."--Baas, Jacquelynn. "John McHale" In The Independent Group: postwar Britain and the aesthetics of plenty. Edited by David Robbins. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, c1990

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A photographic copy of item 2a in the present collection.

did.physdesc: 1 collage book (unfinished) : magazine tear sheets cut in strips and glued to heavy-stock paper ; 25 x 42 cm
1 collage book (unfinished) : magazine tear sheets cut in strips and glued to heavy-stock paper ; 30 x 46 cm
1 collage : brown kraft paper, gray construction paper and yellow and black coated papers ; 19.5 x 59 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of works called Transistors. These works serve as visual analogs to the processing of information. The invention of the transistor in 1948 led to the development of the electronic computer, as well as smaller radios and other communication devices. McHale's works relate to such new technological phenomena.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : black ink on white paper and aluminum foil ; 47.5 x 39.5 cm, in framed 65 x 56.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : orange yellow and white construction paper with black fiber-tip pen on white wove paper ; 60.5 x 46 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : magazine and newspaper tear sheets and black construction paper on wove paper ; 56.5 x 46 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : magazine tear sheets on green construction paper, mounted on purple paper ; 65 x 47.5

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 97 items : magazine and newspaper tear sheets ; 26 x 10 - 27 x 36 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Periodicals used include Life , London Sunday Times , Look and Saturday Evening Post . Includes "A 100 mile high portrait of Earth" (from Look ) and an almost complete copy of Life, April 11, 1955. One advertisement in this issue is noted as being used in Richard Hamilton's A-A-AH .

did.physdesc: 106 items : newspaper and magazine clippings, tear sheets, articles and a business card ; 10 x 15 - 27 x 36 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Periodicals used include Madison Avenue , New Yorker , and Time . One of the photographs depicts McHale's Virginia Imported , 1957.

did.physdesc: 7 drawings : graphite on paper ; 30 x 24 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: associated with palette #2

did.physdesc: 7 items : collage pieces from tear sheets and paper; two photographs, one ink drawing (folded)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Associated with palette #2. The photographs are probably related to the collage book project. The drawing illustrated a plan for the book format.

did.physdesc: 127 items : magazine and newspaper tear sheets, construction paper

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The last working palette, although a number of tear sheets are dated earlier (1950s). Some are prepared for collage.

did.physdesc: 3 items : magazine and newspaper tear sheets, construction paper

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Partially assembled collage parts, pieces of construction paper, oversized palette material. Includes a portion of the Houston Chronicle , section 1, for September 1, 1977.

did.physdesc: 5 items : magazine and newspaper tear sheets, photograph

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes tearsheet from tackboard in John McHale's London studio c.1956, as depicted in the Sam Lambert photograph.

did.physdesc: 1 collage : paper collage mounted on masonite board ; 121.6 x 110.2 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For a full description and image of this work, see the online catalogue of the Yale Center for British Art.

did.physdesc: 1 painting : oil and paper collage on canvas ; 152.4 x 101.6 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For a full description and image of this work, see the online catalogue of the Yale Center for British Art.

did.physdesc: 1 painting : oil and paper collage on canvas ; 152.1 x 101.3 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: For a full description and image of this work, see the online catalogue of the Yale Center for British Art.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black ink (possibly on japan paper) ; 56.5 x 44.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One of three in a series of geometric drawings, 1952.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black ink on wove paper with deckled edges ; 60 x 43 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One of three in a series of geometric drawings, 1952.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black ink with collage, blue-gray construction paper on wove paper ; 58.5 x 46 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One of three in a series of geometric drawings, 1952.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : pen and black ink with gray wash ; 53.3 x 38

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : pen and brush, black ink and gray wash ; sheet 91 x 58.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : pen and black ink (possibly on japan paper) ; 28 x 21 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aluminum was a lightweight construction material increasingly used in the 1950s by architects. It is sometimes associated with the New Pragmaticist architecture being built in Britain in these years. McHale's 'head-collages' of the 1950s, although they bear a superficial resemblance to Paolozzi's works, have been better described as images of the 'media-fed man' (Robert Freeman) -- figures that are "defined by what they consume." This consumerist conception of collage differs somewhat from its predecessors.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black and red paint on shiny wove paper ; sheet 91.5 x 59.5 cm
1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; 54 x 38 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brush and black poster paint and black fiber-tip pen on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; sheet 76 x 54 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen with red shiny construction paper on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; sheet 77 x 54 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen on shiny tearsheet from yearbook (photos on verso) ; sheet 78 x 54 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : brown fiber-tip pen on wove paper ; sheet 97 x 64 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : red and black fiber-tip pen on wove paper ; sheet 94 x 63.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: 1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen on wove paper ; sheet 94 x 63.5 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From a series of large scale drawings using various pre-Columbian (?) and geometric motifs. The yearbook tearsheet on which these motifs are drawn is almost certainly of American origin.

did.physdesc: One sketchpad, with disbound leaves and 24 additional laid-in drawings; includes drawings in pen and ink or graphite; also includes four photocopies of drawings in pen and ink.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The majority of these drawings are probably preliminary sketches to the larger fiber-tip pen/poster paint drawings in the collection, although a few drawings from this collection of sketches seem to be unrelated. The ball point pen drawings in this series are revisions of earlier graphite sketches. There is also a drawing which is derived from a magazine tear-sheet in Palette #2; in the subsequent sketch the design is further abstracted and reintegrated back into a 'Head' composed of pre-Columbian motifs.

did.unittitle: Sheets prepared by McHale for discussion of lecture by E.W. 'Bingo' Meyer, entitled "Probability and Information Theory and their Application to the Visual Arts" given March 8, 1955, to the Independent Group at the Institute of Contemporary Arts

did.physdesc: 1 diagram : black and red fiber-tip pen with black construction paper ; sheet 58 x 91 cm
1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen ; sheet 91 x 58 cm
1 drawing : black and red fiber-tip pen with blue-gray construction paper and magazine tear-sheets ; sheet 58 x 91 cm
1 graph : black, yellow, and blue fiber-tip pen and graphite ; sheet 91 x 58 cm
1 table : black, red, and green fiber-tip pen and blue-gray construction paper ; 91 x 58 cm

did.unittitle: Frequency of Occurrence of Letters in English in 1000 Letters
did.physdesc: 1 chart : black and red fiber-tip pen with graphite ; sheet 58 x 91 cm
1 drawing : black fiber-tip pen with yellow and black construction paper ; sheet 91 x 58 cm

did.unittitle: Paintings, Drawings, Sculptures by Jean Dubuffet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: At the Institute of Contemporary Arts Gallery, 17-18 Dover Street, London W1, 30 March - 30 April.

did.physdesc: 1 poster : screen print with red and black ink ; sheet 51 x 76 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The poster, designed by McHale, was produced for an exhibition at the Whitechapel Art Gallery, August 9-September 9, 1956.
The exhibition was divided into various collaborative groups of architects and artists, each of which produced its own poster. This poster was likely produced independently from the group posters.

corpname.part: This Is Tomorrow (1956 : Whitechapel Art Gallery)

did.physdesc: 1 poster :; screen print with black ink ; 77 x 51 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A screen print of Richard Hamilton's Just what is it that makes today's homes so different, so appealing? , produced for an exhibition at the Whitechapel Art Gallery, August 9-September 9, 1956.

The exhibition was divided into various collaborative groups of architects and artists. Each of these groups produced its own poster for the exhibition. John McHale was in Group Two with Richard Hamilton and John Voelcker.

corpname.part: This Is Tomorrow (1956 : Whitechapel Art Gallery)

did.physdesc: 1 exhibition poster : screen print with black ink

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster by Nigel Henderson, for the exhibition at the Whitechapel Art Gallery, August 9-September 9, 1956. The design features an altered photograph of Peter Smithson, Eduardo Paolozzi, Alison Smithson, and Nigel Henderson.
The exhibition was divided into various collaborative groups' of architects and artists. Each of these groups produced its own poster for the exhibition.

corpname.part: This Is Tomorrow (1956 : Whitechapel Art Gallery)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster for an exhibition at the Institute of Contemporary Arts, September 6-October 13, 1962. Credit: Gordon House and Kelpra Studio Ltd., London

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poster by Toshihiro Katayama, advertising a lecture by John McHale at the Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts, Harvard University, December 12, 1968.

persname.part: Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts

did.unittitle: Catalogs, periodicals, ephemera, and small posters
Institute of Contemporary Arts : sculpture exhibition arranged by Lawrence Alloway

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Lists works by Robert Adams, Kenneth Armitage, Trevor Bates, Reg Butler, Lynn Chadwick, Geoffrey Clarke, Elizabeth Frink, Barbara Hepworth, John McHale, Bernard Meadows, Henry Moore, Eduardo Paolozzi, William Turnbull, and Rosemary Young.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photocopy of the original brochure. "Exhibition organized by Lawrence Alloway; 13 October - 20 November 1954; London: Institute of Contempoary Arts, 1954."--Leaf [2]. With a list of the 83 items exhibited, including two collages books by John McHale: Palimpcestuous (no. 81) and Secret Life of a Talisman (no. 82).

did.unittitle: The Independent Group, schedule of seminars for Spring 1955 session
did.physdesc: 2 sheets ([2] pages) : mimeograph typescript ; 33 x 21 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Seminar speakers include: Lawrence Alloway, Reyner Banham, Frank Cordell, Gillo Dorfles, Richard Hamilton, Anthony Hill, Donald Holmes, John McHale, E.W. Meyer, Eduardo Paolozzi, Tony del Renzio, and Peter Smithson.

did.physdesc: 1 folded sheet : illustrations ; 16 x 37 cm, folded 16 x 13 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brochure. Subtitle: "27 November-15 December 1956. ICA Library, 17-18 Dover Street, London, W1." With an enumeration of the collages: "1. Zebra. 2. Puffhead. 3. Figurehead. 4. Rosehead. 5. Flexible head. 6. Chocolate head. 7. Dial. 8. Maphead. 9. Silverhead. 10. Furhead. 11. Olive head."

did.unittitle: John McHale and Magda Cordell in studio, with tack board bearing tear sheets from McHale's Palette #4
did.physdesc: 1 photograph : gelatin silver print ; 17 x 16 cm

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The photograph is a reproduction, not the original.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Exhibition announcement, designed by Toni Del Renzio. "Arranged by Lawrence Alloway with the co-operation of the ICA at the O'Hana Gallery, 3 Carlos Place, Grosvenor Square W1, December 6 to 21 …"

did.unittitle: 3 Collagists: New work by E.L.T Mesens, John McHale, Gwyther Irwin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Small exhibition poster. Institute of Contemporary Arts, 5-29 November 1958.

persname.part: Mesens, E. L. T., 1903-1971 (Edouard Léon Théodore)

did.physdesc: 1 folded sheet ; 26 x 26 cm, folded to 10 x 26 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brochure for an exhibition at the Institute of Contemporary Arts, 1958. Includes artists' statements by Irwin, McHale, and Mesens.

persname.part: Mesens, E. L. T., 1903-1971 (Edouard Léon Théodore)

did.unittitle: Class of '59 : Magda Cordell, Eduardo Paolozzi, John McHale : paintings, sculpture, collages

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Brochure for an exhibition at The Union, Cambridge, February 7-19, 1959. Arranged by the Cambridge Contemporary Art Trust. One side of the sheet serves as a small poster. The verso includes a checklist and a commentary by Lawrence Alloway.

did.unittitle: Lady Clare : a review : volume XLIV, June 1959

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "The Human Image" by Robert Freeman, pages 6-9, with commentary on the work of Magda Cordell and John McHale; and "Images of the mass media" by John McHale, pages 10-11.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Presented by the Blackfriars Settlement ... at the Institute of Contemporary Arts ... 27 April to 12 May 1962 … Louis le Brocquey, Anne Buchanan, John Ernest, Andrew Forge, John McHale, Sidney Nolan, John Plumb, William Scott, Peter Stroud, Joe Tilson, and William Turnbull." With text describing the Blackfriars Settlement.

did.physdesc: 1 folded sheet (3 panels) ; 21 x 60 cm, folded to 21 x 20 cm

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes "The Plastic Parthenon" by John McHale, pages 4-11.

McHale writes: "The future of art seems no longer to lie with the creation of enduring masterworks but with defining alternative cultural strategies, through a series of communicative gestures in multi-media forms. As art and non-art become interchangeable, and the masterwork may only be a reel of punched or magnetized tape, the artist defines art less through any intrinsic value of the art object than by furnishing new conceptualities of life style and orientation ..."
